analogy: Think of "Air" through the chapter's own opening images: Our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of air called atmosphere. All living beings on this earth depend on the atmosphere for their survival. It provides us the air we breathe and protects us from the harmful effects of the sun’s rays. Without this... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: COMPOSITION OF THE ATMOSPHERE, 22 OUR ENVIRONMENT, STRUCTURE OF THE ATMOSPHERE, WEATHER AND CLIMATE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Air is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of air called atmosphere.

The text explains that all living beings on this earth depend on the atmosphere for their survival.

The text explains that it provides us the air we breathe and protects us from the harmful effects of the sun’s rays.

The text explains that without this blanket of protection, we would be baked alive by the heat of the sun during day and get frozen during night.

The text explains that so it is this mass of air that has made the temperature on the earth liveable.

The text explains that cOMPOSITION OF THE ATMOSPHERE Do you know that the air we take in while breathing is actually a mixture of many gases?

The text explains that nitrogen and oxygen are two gases which make up the bulk of the atmosphere.

The text explains that carbon dioxide, helium, ozone, argon and hydrogen are found in lesser quantities.

The text explains that apart from these gases, tiny dust particles are also present in the air.

The text explains that the pie chart gives you the percentage of different constituents of air (Fig.

The text explains that nitrogen is the most plentiful gas in the air.

The text explains that when we inhale, we take some amount of nitrogen into our lungs and exhale it.

The text explains that carbon dioxide released in the atmosphere creates a green house effect by trapping the heat radiated from the earth.

The text explains that it is therefore called a greenhouse gas and without it the earth would have been too cold to live in.

The text explains that however, when its level in the atmosphere increases due to factory smoke or car fumes, the heat retained increases the temperature of the earth.

The text explains that this rise in temperature causes the snow in coldest parts of the world to melt.

The text explains that as a result, the increased volume of carbon dioxide is affecting the earth’s weather and climate.

The text explains that 22 OUR ENVIRONMENT STRUCTURE OF THE ATMOSPHERE Our atmosphere is divided into five layers starting from the earth’s sur face.

The text explains that one important feature of stratosphere is that it contains a layer of ozone gas.

The text explains that thermosphere: In thermosphere temperature rises very rapidly with increasing height.

The text explains that you will be surprised to know that the earth receives only 1 in 2,000,000,000 parts of the sun’s energy.

The text explains that in fact, radio waves transmitted from the earth are reflected back to the earth by this layer.

The text explains that wEATHER AND CLIMATE “Is it going to rain today?” “Will it be bright and sunny today?” How many times have we heard this from anxious cricket fans speculating the fate of a One Day match?

The text explains that temperature The temperature you feel everyday is the temperataure of the atmosphere.

The text explains that the degree of hotness and coldness of the air is known as temperature.

The text explains that the temperature of the atmosphere changes not only between day and night but also from season to season.

The text explains that an important factor that influences the distribution of temperature is insolation.

The text explains that insolation is the incoming solar energy intercepted by the earth.

The text explains that wind Vane: Shows the direction of the wind Rain Gauge: Measures the amount of rainfall Thermometer: Measures the temperature Barometre: Measures atmospheric pressure Fig.

The text explains that 24 OUR ENVIRONMENT temperature decreases in the same manner.

The text explains that if the earth’s temperature rises too high, it would become too warm for some crops to grow.

The text explains that temperature in cities is much higher than that of villages.

The text explains that also, the crowded high rise buildings of the cities trap the warm air and thus raise the temperature of the cities.

The text explains that air Pressure You will be surprised to know that air above us presses us with a great force on our bodies.

The text explains that this is because the air presses us from all directions and our body exerts a counter pressure.

The text explains that air pressure is defined as the pressure exerted by the weight of air on the earth’s surface.

The text explains that as we go up the layers of atmosphere, the pressure falls rapidly.

The text explains that the air pressure is highest at sea level and decreases with height.

The text explains that horizontally the distribution of air pressure is influenced by temperature of air at a given place.

The text explains that in areas where temperature is high the air gets heated and rises.

The text explains that low pressure is associated with cloudy skies and wet weather.

The text explains that in areas having lower temperature, the air is cold.

The text explains that heavy air sinks and creates a high pressure area.

The text explains that high pressure is associated with clear and sunny skies.

The text explains that the air always moves from high pressure areas to low pressure areas.

The text explains that example of local wind blowing in summer in northern india 4.

The text explains that it is ....... time when sun is overhead Across 6.

The text explains that an Indian tree having extraordinary quality of providing oxygen round the clock 8.

The text explains that gas present in atmosphere occupying only 0.03% by volume 11.

The text explains that an indian tree valued highly for medicinal properties 18.

The text explains that (i) Solve this Crossword puzzle with the help of given clues:

The text explains that sunny day Day Weather (ii) Make a weather calendar for one week.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Environment, Chapter 4: Air

revision_bullets: Our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of air called atmosphere. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
All living beings on this earth depend on the atmosphere for their survival. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
It provides us the air we breathe and protects us from the harmful effects of the sun’s rays. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
Without this blanket of protection, we would be baked alive by the heat of the sun during day and get frozen during night. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
So it is this mass of air that has made the temperature on the earth liveable. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
COMPOSITION OF THE ATMOSPHERE Do you know that the air we take in while breathing is actually a mixture of many gases? (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
Nitrogen and oxygen are two gases which make up the bulk of the atmosphere. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
Carbon dioxide, helium, ozone, argon and hydrogen are found in lesser quantities. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
Apart from these gases, tiny dust particles are also present in the air. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)
The pie chart gives you the percentage of different constituents of air (Fig. (NCERT Class 7 Our Environment Ch4 pg 1)

Do not add outside examples to Air; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
